ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA is being built as a distributed array of radio telescopes across two sites: SKA-Low in Western Australia and SKA-Mid in South Africa. Each telescope provides complementary capabilities across a wide range of frequencies and offers multiple observing modes to support diverse science goals.

p: Array Configuration: 512 stations, each comprising 256 phased-array dipoles. Stations are distributed across a region extending to ~65 km from the core.

ul.li: Continuum imaging across the low-frequency band
Spectral-line observations (HI, OH masers, recombination lines)
Transient detection and characterization
Pulsar searches and timing
Solar and ionospheric monitoring
Beamforming for tied-array pulsar observations

p: Array Configuration: 197 dishes distributed across multiple clusters. Core concentration at Karoo site; extended baseline to ~150 km.

ul.li: High-frequency continuum imaging with arcsecond to sub-arcsecond resolution
Spectral-line observations (CO, other molecular lines)
Pulsar timing and polarimetry
VLBI (Very Long Baseline Interferometry) for ultra-high angular resolution
Transient detection via imaging and commensal modes
Rapid target-of-opportunity observations

p: The SKA supports multiple simultaneous observing modes via commensal observing, where multiple science programmes can access the same data stream. Key modes include:

ul.li: Continuum Imaging: Maps of source intensity across frequency
Spectral-Line Observations: High-resolution frequency studies of spectral features
Pulsar Search: Detection of new pulsars via tied-array beams
Pulsar Timing: Precision timing of known pulsars for gravitational-wave detection and tests of relativity
Transient Detection: Rapid identification of variable and explosive sources
VLBI: Ultra-high resolution observations in collaboration with global networks

p: The SKA's broad frequency coverage and multiple observing modes support research across all major radio astronomy disciplines:

ul.li: Cosmology & Large-Scale Structure: HI surveys, dark energy, galaxy evolution
Compact Objects: Pulsars, magnetars, black hole accretion
Active Galaxies & Jets: AGN, VLBI studies of jets and cores
Stars & Star Formation: Masers, OH emission, molecular lines
Transient & Time-Domain: Supernovae, GRBs, FRBs
Solar & Ionospheric Physics: Solar bursts, ionospheric disturbances
Fundamental Physics: Pulsar timing arrays, tests of gravity

ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA's capabilities are extensive. When planning your proposal, consider which frequency range, observing mode, and sensitivity requirements best match your science goals. See the Proposing section for guidance on selecting the right telescope and mode for your project.
